questions.question: What is the foundational principle governing the availability of specific performance as an equitable remedy?

options.A: Specific performance is available as of right whenever a valid and enforceable contract exists
options.B: Specific performance is a discretionary remedy, only available where damages would be inadequate
options.C: Specific performance is only available in contracts involving real property
options.D: Specific performance is the primary remedy for breach of contract and damages are secondary

questions.explanation: Specific performance is a discretionary equitable remedy that compels a party to perform their contractual obligations. It is not available as of right — it is only granted where the common law remedy of damages would be inadequate to compensate the claimant. The court exercises its discretion having regard to all the circumstances, including the conduct of the parties and the nature of the subject matter.

questions.question: Why is specific performance most commonly sought — and most readily granted — in contracts for the sale of land?

options.A: Because land transactions are always high-value and damages are commercially insufficient
options.B: Because each piece of land is considered unique, making damages an inadequate substitute for the specific property contracted for
options.C: Because the Land Registry requires a court order before any transfer can be registered
options.D: Because land contracts are governed exclusively by equity rather than common law

questions.explanation: Specific performance is most commonly sought in contracts for the sale of land because equity regards each piece of land as unique — no two properties are identical in terms of location, character, and value. Since damages cannot adequately compensate a buyer deprived of the specific property they contracted to purchase, specific performance is available almost as of right in land transactions, subject to the usual equitable defences.

questions.question: Under what circumstances might specific performance be available in a contract for the sale of goods?

options.A: Specific performance is never available for contracts for the sale of goods — it is limited to land
options.B: Specific performance may be granted where the goods are unique or rare, such that substitute goods cannot readily be obtained on the open market
options.C: Specific performance is available for all goods contracts where the purchase price exceeds a certain statutory threshold
options.D: Specific performance is available for goods only where the seller has already delivered and is seeking payment

questions.explanation: As a general rule, specific performance is not available for contracts for the sale of ordinary goods because substitute goods can usually be obtained on the open market and damages will suffice. However, where the goods are unique or rare — such as a specific painting, an antique, or a one-of-a-kind collectible — equity may grant specific performance because damages cannot adequately compensate for the loss of the particular item.

questions.question: In Ryan v Mutual Tontine Westminster Chambers Association [1893], the court refused specific performance of a contract to provide a porter. What principle does this case illustrate?

options.A: Specific performance is not available to enforce employment obligations of any kind
options.B: Specific performance will not be granted where enforcement would require constant judicial supervision
options.C: Specific performance requires the defendant to be a corporate body rather than an individual
options.D: Specific performance cannot be granted in contracts for the provision of services in residential premises

questions.explanation: Ryan v Mutual Tontine Westminster Chambers Association [1893] is the leading case for the principle that specific performance will not be granted where enforcing the order would require ongoing or constant judicial supervision. In that case, the court refused to order the provision of a porter because monitoring the quality and continuity of the service would require continuous court involvement, which equity will not undertake. This remains a key bar to the remedy.

questions.question: Why will equity generally refuse to grant specific performance of a contract of personal service?

options.A: Because personal service contracts are void at common law and cannot be enforced in any court
options.B: Because compelling a person to work for another would be tantamount to forced labour, which equity will not countenance
options.C: Because personal service contracts always contain implied terms that render specific performance inappropriate
options.D: Because the Chancery Amendment Act 1858 expressly prohibits specific performance in personal service cases

questions.explanation: Equity will not grant specific performance of a contract of personal service because compelling a person to work for another against their will would be tantamount to forced or compulsory labour. This is a fundamental bar rooted in the principle of personal liberty. Instead, the court may award damages for breach, or in appropriate cases, award damages in lieu of specific performance under the Chancery Amendment Act 1858 (Lord Cairns' Act).

questions.question: The Chancery Amendment Act 1858, known as Lord Cairns' Act, is relevant to specific performance proceedings. What does it allow?

options.A: It allows the court to compel performance of personal service contracts in exceptional circumstances
options.B: It allows the court to award damages in lieu of specific performance where specific performance is refused
options.C: It removes the discretionary character of specific performance and makes it available as of right in land cases
options.D: It restricts the circumstances in which specific performance can be granted to those expressly listed in the Act

questions.explanation: Section 2 of the Chancery Amendment Act 1858 (Lord Cairns' Act) gives the court a statutory jurisdiction to award damages in lieu of — or in addition to — specific performance or injunction. This is significant because it extends the court's power to award damages in cases where at common law no damages would be recoverable (e.g., where the wrong had not yet occurred), and where equity has jurisdiction but refuses specific performance.

questions.question: The traditional 'want of mutuality' doctrine held that specific performance would not be granted to one party unless it was also available to the other. How did Price v Strange [1978] modify this approach?

options.A: Price v Strange abolished the mutuality requirement entirely, making it irrelevant to specific performance
options.B: Price v Strange held that mutuality should be assessed at the time of the hearing, not at the time of the contract, adopting a more flexible approach
options.C: Price v Strange restricted the mutuality requirement to cases involving land transactions only
options.D: Price v Strange held that mutuality is only relevant where the defendant raises it as an express defence

questions.explanation: Price v Strange [1978] adopted a more flexible approach to the mutuality requirement. Rather than insisting that specific performance must be available to both parties at the time of contracting, the Court of Appeal held that the relevant question is whether mutuality exists at the time of the hearing. This more modern approach allows the court to grant specific performance even in cases where, at the time of contract formation, the remedy would not have been available to one of the parties.

questions.question: In Patel v Ali [1984], specific performance of a contract for the sale of a house was refused on grounds of hardship. What does this case establish about the hardship defence?

options.A: Hardship to the claimant is sufficient to trigger the hardship defence and justify refusal of the remedy
options.B: Hardship arising after the contract and not foreseeable at the time of contracting can justify refusal of specific performance to the vendor
options.C: Hardship is only relevant where the defendant is a natural person; it cannot be raised by corporate defendants
options.D: The hardship defence only applies where the property in question is the defendant's principal private residence

questions.explanation: Patel v Ali [1984] established that where circumstances have arisen since the contract was made that were not foreseeable at the time of contracting, and where granting specific performance would cause severe hardship to the defendant, the court may refuse the order. In that case, the vendor had become disabled and had a young child to care for — circumstances that arose after the contract and that would make it extremely difficult for her to vacate the property. The hardship was not self-induced and justified the exercise of discretion against granting the remedy.

questions.question: Which of the following equitable defences is applicable to a claim for specific performance?

options.A: Contributory negligence
options.B: Laches, clean hands, and unconscionability
options.C: Ex turpi causa and illegality only
options.D: Estoppel and statute of limitations only

questions.explanation: As a discretionary equitable remedy, specific performance is subject to all the standard equitable defences. These include: laches (unreasonable delay in bringing the claim), clean hands (the claimant must not have acted inequitably in relation to the subject matter of the claim), and unconscionability (where granting the remedy would be unconscionable in all the circumstances). Contributory negligence is a common law defence inapplicable in this context.

questions.question: Roberts v O'Neill [1983] is an important Irish authority on specific performance. What did the Supreme Court emphasise in that case?

options.A: That specific performance should be granted automatically in all land sale contracts without further analysis
options.B: That the discretionary nature of the remedy requires a thorough analysis of all relevant factors before the court will grant or refuse it
options.C: That specific performance is not available in Ireland and has been replaced by statutory compensation
options.D: That mutuality is the primary consideration and all other factors are secondary

questions.explanation: In Roberts v O'Neill [1983], the Supreme Court emphasised the discretionary nature of specific performance as an equitable remedy and stressed the importance of conducting a thorough analysis of all relevant factors before deciding whether to exercise the discretion in favour of the applicant. The case confirms that Irish courts will engage carefully with the circumstances of each case rather than treating specific performance as automatic even in land transactions.

questions.question: A purchaser contracts to buy a rare nineteenth-century Irish landscape painting from a gallery. The gallery later refuses to complete the sale. Which remedy is the purchaser most likely to seek and why?

options.A: Damages, because the purchaser can acquire a replacement painting from another source
options.B: Specific performance, because the painting is a unique item and damages would be inadequate
options.C: Rescission, because the gallery's refusal amounts to a fundamental breach of contract
options.D: Rectification, to correct the contract to include a more favourable price

questions.explanation: The purchaser would most likely seek specific performance. The painting is a unique and rare item — a specific nineteenth-century Irish landscape — and no identical substitute is available on the open market. Damages would therefore be inadequate to compensate for the loss of this particular item. This brings the case within the exception to the general rule that specific performance is unavailable for goods contracts, as the goods in question are unique.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a recognised bar to the grant of specific performance?

options.A: The contract requires constant judicial supervision to enforce
options.B: The contract is one of personal service
options.C: The claimant has an adequate remedy in damages
options.D: The contract was not reduced to writing and was concluded orally

questions.explanation: The absence of a written contract is not itself a recognised bar to specific performance in all cases. In land transactions, for example, the absence of writing might engage the Statute of Frauds, but part performance may allow equity to enforce an oral agreement. The established bars to specific performance are: the adequacy of damages (option C), the requirement for constant supervision (option A), and the personal service nature of the contract (option B). These bars reflect the fundamental principles underpinning the discretionary exercise of the remedy.

questions.question: What is the effect of laches as a defence to a specific performance claim?

options.A: Laches extinguishes the underlying cause of action at common law
options.B: Laches may cause the court to refuse specific performance as a matter of equitable discretion where the claimant has unreasonably delayed in bringing the claim to the defendant's prejudice
options.C: Laches only applies where the defendant has changed their position in reliance on the delay
options.D: Laches operates as an absolute time bar after twelve years, equivalent to the limitation period for simple contracts

questions.explanation: Laches is an equitable defence based on unreasonable delay. Where a claimant has unreasonably delayed in seeking specific performance and that delay has caused prejudice to the defendant, the court may exercise its discretion to refuse the remedy. Unlike a statutory limitation period, laches is not subject to a fixed time period — the question is whether, in all the circumstances, the delay was unreasonable and caused prejudice. It is a ground for refusing discretionary relief, not for extinguishing the underlying cause of action at common law.

questions.question: The 'clean hands' maxim operates as a defence to specific performance. Which of the following best explains its application in this context?

options.A: The claimant must not have committed any wrongdoing of any kind at any point in their life
options.B: The claimant's own inequitable or unconscionable conduct in relation to the subject matter of the claim will disentitle them to equitable relief
options.C: The defendant must prove that the claimant committed fraud before the clean hands defence can be raised
options.D: Clean hands requires the claimant to have paid any outstanding taxes before equity will assist them

questions.explanation: The 'clean hands' maxim ('he who comes to equity must come with clean hands') requires the claimant to have behaved equitably in relation to the subject matter of the specific performance claim. If the claimant has acted unconscionably, dishonestly, or in breach of their own obligations in relation to the same transaction, the court may refuse to grant specific performance in its discretion. The misconduct must be connected to the subject of the claim — unrelated wrongdoing will not typically bar the remedy.

questions.question: A vendor of a house contracts with a purchaser. Before completion, the vendor discovers that a third party has registered a judgment mortgage against the property. The vendor argues that specific performance should not be granted because the property's title is now encumbered. How should this be analysed?

options.A: The judgment mortgage automatically terminates the contract and specific performance is no longer available
options.B: The purchaser can still seek specific performance with an abatement of the purchase price to reflect the encumbrance, or the vendor may be required to discharge the judgment mortgage before completion
options.C: The encumbrance means the vendor has committed fraudulent misrepresentation, making rescission the only available remedy
options.D: Specific performance cannot be granted where the vendor is a party to litigation

questions.explanation: The existence of an encumbrance on title does not automatically defeat a specific performance claim. The court can fashion appropriate relief — including ordering specific performance subject to an abatement (reduction) of the purchase price to reflect the value of the encumbrance, or requiring the vendor to discharge the encumbrance prior to or contemporaneously with completion. Courts seek to give effect to bargains wherever possible, and will use their equitable jurisdiction to do justice between the parties.

questions.question: A theatre company has contracted with a celebrated actor to perform in a play for a six-month run. The actor refuses to perform and accepts a role with a rival theatre. What remedy, if any, can the theatre company pursue?

options.A: Specific performance — to compel the actor to perform in the play as contracted
options.B: Specific performance is unavailable; the theatre company must instead pursue damages for breach of contract
options.C: Specific performance is unavailable; however, an injunction restraining the actor from performing for the rival theatre may be available
options.D: The theatre company has no remedy because entertainment contracts cannot be enforced in equity

questions.explanation: Specific performance of a personal service contract (such as a contract to perform as an actor) will not be granted — compelling the actor to perform would be tantamount to forced labour, which equity refuses. However, where the contract contains an express or implied negative covenant (not to perform elsewhere), the court may grant an injunction restraining the actor from performing for a rival theatre. This is an important practical distinction: equity will not compel performance but may restrain breach of a negative stipulation in a personal service contract.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between damages and specific performance?

options.A: Specific performance is always preferable to damages and should be ordered in all breach of contract cases
options.B: Specific performance is only available where the claimant elects it; damages are always available as an alternative
options.C: Specific performance is only available where damages are inadequate — if damages are an adequate remedy, the court will not grant specific performance
options.D: Damages and specific performance can never both be available for the same breach — the claimant must choose one

questions.explanation: The fundamental requirement for specific performance is that the common law remedy of damages must be inadequate. This is the primary threshold question: if damages would adequately compensate the claimant, specific performance will not be granted. It is only where the subject matter is unique (as with land), or where damages are impossible to calculate, or where the position cannot be restored by a monetary award, that equity will intervene with specific performance.

questions.question: A builder contracts to construct a unique bespoke home for a client but abandons the project halfway through. The client seeks specific performance to compel completion of the building. What is the likely outcome?

options.A: Specific performance will be granted because the house is unique
options.B: Specific performance is likely to be refused because it would require constant judicial supervision to monitor the quality of the building work
options.C: Specific performance will be granted automatically because the subject matter is real property
options.D: Specific performance will be refused because construction contracts are personal service contracts

questions.explanation: A contract for the construction of a building is subject to the constant supervision bar established in Ryan v Mutual Tontine Westminster Chambers Association [1893]. Compelling a builder to complete construction to the required standard would require the court to supervise the quality, progress, and standard of the ongoing building work — something equity will not undertake. The client's best remedy would be damages to cover the cost of having the work completed by a substitute contractor.

questions.question: Specific performance is described as a 'discretionary' remedy. Which of the following factors might cause a court to exercise its discretion AGAINST granting the order, even where the technical requirements are satisfied?

options.A: The fact that the defendant is a company rather than an individual
options.B: The claimant's own delay in seeking relief (laches), inequitable conduct (unclean hands), or disproportionate hardship to the defendant
options.C: The fact that the contract was concluded without legal representation on both sides
options.D: The fact that the property has increased in value since the date of the contract

questions.explanation: The discretionary nature of specific performance means the court can refuse the remedy even where the technical requirements are met. Factors that may lead to refusal include: laches (unreasonable delay by the claimant), unclean hands (the claimant's own inequitable conduct), hardship to the defendant that arose after the contract and was not foreseeable (as in Patel v Ali [1984]), and any other circumstances that make it inequitable to compel performance. The fact that a property has increased in value is not a bar — indeed it strengthens the claimant's case for the remedy.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies when specific performance may be granted 'almost as of right' in Irish law?

options.A: In all consumer contracts for goods or services
options.B: In contracts for the sale of land, because land is regarded as unique and damages are inherently inadequate
options.C: In contracts where both parties are legally represented and the contract is executed as a deed
options.D: In any contract where the purchase price exceeds €1,000,000

questions.explanation: Specific performance is available almost as of right in contracts for the sale of land. Because equity regards every piece of land as unique — no two properties are identical in terms of location, character, and value — damages are inherently inadequate to compensate a purchaser for the loss of the specific property contracted for. In land transactions, therefore, specific performance will typically be granted subject only to the equitable defences such as laches, clean hands, hardship, and want of mutuality.

questions.question: In an FE-1 exam question, a vendor of a residential property refuses to complete a sale contract, claiming personal difficulties. The purchaser seeks specific performance. Which of the following is the BEST structured approach to answering this question?

options.A: Advise that specific performance will automatically be granted because it involves land
options.B: Analyse: (1) is specific performance available — are damages adequate?; (2) are there any bars — supervision, personal service, want of mutuality, hardship?; (3) are there equitable defences — laches, clean hands?; (4) exercise of discretion
options.C: Advise the purchaser to seek an injunction instead of specific performance as it is easier to obtain
options.D: Advise that the vendor's personal difficulties constitute a complete defence to any claim for specific performance

questions.explanation: The best approach in an FE-1 specific performance question follows a structured analysis: (1) establish that damages are inadequate (land is unique); (2) consider any bars — constant supervision (not relevant here), personal service (not applicable), want of mutuality (consider price v Strange [1978]), hardship (consider Patel v Ali [1984] — were the vendor's difficulties foreseeable?); (3) consider equitable defences — laches, clean hands; (4) conclude on how the court is likely to exercise its discretion. This structured approach demonstrates command of the law and earns marks at each stage.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about specific performance is INCORRECT?

options.A: Specific performance may be refused where the claimant is guilty of laches
options.B: Specific performance is only available where damages would be an inadequate remedy
options.C: Specific performance may be granted for contracts for the sale of unique goods
options.D: Specific performance is a common law remedy and is therefore available as of right upon proof of breach

questions.explanation: Option D is incorrect. Specific performance is an equitable remedy, not a common law remedy. As an equitable remedy, it is discretionary — it is not available as of right upon mere proof of breach. The court must be satisfied that damages would be inadequate, that no bars apply, and that there is no equitable defence. Options A (laches is a valid defence), B (inadequacy of damages is the threshold requirement), and C (specific performance can be granted for unique goods) are all correct statements.
